languages._comment_multi_stage: lang_node.py's make_dockerfile() goes multi-stage (2026-07-11) whenever there's an actual toolchain to discard: a native node-gyp compile (sodium-native/bcrypt/argon2's python3/make/g++[/autoconf/automake/libtool]), liboqs-node's own heavier cmake/ninja/git/libssl-dev/pkg-config build (~250MB+), or NestJS's TypeScript compiler (needed to produce app.js, never to run it). The `builder` stage keeps the toolchain and does the (only) `npm install` (+ `npx tsc` for NestJS), then `npm prune --omit=dev` removes devDependency packages from the ALREADY-installed tree in place -- deliberately NOT a second `npm install --omit=dev` in the final stage, since for native production dependencies (sodium-native etc. are `dependencies`, not `devDependencies`) that would just re-trigger the same native compile there, needing the toolchain a second time and defeating the point. The final stage starts fresh and only `COPY --from=builder`s the pruned `node_modules` (+ compiled `app.js` for NestJS, + the self-contained `/opt/liboqs-node` tree for liboqs-node) -- no apt-get at all. Confirmed working end-to-end (clean `/version` responses, plus a live Kyber768/ML-KEM-768-equivalent keypair generated for liboqs-node) across a native-compile combo, a plain combo, NestJS, and liboqs-node. AdonisJS ('scaffold' kind) is the one framework NOT converted this pass -- its CMD runs Adonis's own dev-mode `ace serve`, which genuinely needs its scaffold's devDependencies (CLI/hot-reload tooling) present at runtime, so the same prune-based approach would break it; despite having by far the largest images in this registry (823MB-1.6GB), fixing it properly means switching to AdonisJS's own documented production `ace build` flow, which needs its own per-major verification pass before being trusted -- flagged as a real follow-up, not silently skipped.
languages._comment_compat: Node's own major numbers are flat integers (4,6,8,...), not major.minor like Go/Python, so the shared is_compatible() 'same-major-bucket' matching can't use '+'/'-' range syntax across majors (a compat entry's leading digit must equal the tested version's leading digit). Compatibility arrays below therefore enumerate every satisfying major literally instead of using 'X+' shorthand. Sub-patch thresholds (e.g. '>=10.16.0') are reduced to their integer major, since each major's Docker tag always resolves to that release line's final/latest patch.
languages._comment_hapi_legacy: Before the @hapi org-wide npm-scope migration, the framework was published as the bare unscoped 'hapi' package -- 19 numbered majors (0 through 18) between 2013 and 2018, confirmed via the npm registry API, overlapping briefly with the new '@hapi/hapi' package at major 17. This is a real, disclosed density tradeoff, not a false 'doesn't work' claim: that many majors in ~5 years reflects loose semver discipline in that npm era (frequent major bumps for changes that wouldn't warrant one today), not 19 genuinely distinct API shapes needing 19 independently-verified templates. The unscoped 'hapi' package is ALSO npm-deprecated ('contains severe security issues and defects') -- deliberately NOT used as a reason to skip it (this project tracks old/vulnerable versions on purpose, e.g. phpseclib 1.x in the PHP registry) -- it's simply not yet version-bucketed in this pass. A real follow-up would verify the pre-17 connection-based API (`server.connection({port})` then `server.start()`, callback-style) and add real buckets the same way Slim's 4 PHP-side eras were split out.

frameworks.name: Express
frameworks.notes: 4.x is the long-lived stable line (2014-present), requires Node >=0.8 (satisfied by everything tracked). 5.x (Sept 2024) requires Node 18+, drops callback-based error handling. 3.x already uses the modern `express()` factory + `res.json()`/`res.send()` shape identical to 4.x/5.x (verified by an actual docker build+run on node:8-slim) -- the commonly-repeated claim that the API break lands at 4.0 is not accurate; 3.x's own real break from 2.x is elsewhere (Connect middleware internals), not the app-bootstrap shape this project's templates touch. 1.x/2.x genuinely differ and are real, build-verified buckets (not reference-only): entry point is `express.createServer()` (removed at 3.0), and responses are `res.send(obj)` (an object short-circuits into JSON automatically) rather than `res.json(obj)` -- confirmed working end-to-end on node:0.10-slim for both 1.0.8 and 2.5.11. `dropped-Node-support` note: 1.x/2.x's own package.json engines fields claim Node <0.4, well below what this project tracks, but npm only warns (not errors) on an engines mismatch and both installed/ran fine on 0.10/0.12 regardless.

frameworks.name: Fastify
frameworks.notes: package.json stopped declaring 'engines' from v3's later patches onward; 4.x/5.x minimums (Node 14.19+, 20+) are per Fastify's documented LTS support policy, not machine-verifiable via npm metadata. Fastify 1.x's dependency tree deterministically hits an npm@3 bug on Node 6 (npm 3.10.10, the version Node 6 bundles) -- `npm install` fails with `ENOTDIR: not a directory, open '.../node_modules/.staging/@types/events-.../package.json'`, a known npm3 '.staging' race that npm5 (bundled from Node 8 onward) doesn't have. Verified directly: fails 5/5 tries on node:6-slim, succeeds 2/2 on node:8-slim, independent of which crypto library is paired with it. Fastify 2.x's smaller/different dependency tree doesn't trigger it (8/8 successes on Node 6), so only major 1 is narrowed to start at Node 8.

frameworks.name: Koa
frameworks.notes: 2.x is the long-lived stable line (2016-2025), uses async/await-shaped middleware (koa-compose wraps every call in Promise.resolve(), so our own generated middleware avoids async -- see lang_node.py notes). 3.x (Apr 2025) requires Node 18+, same middleware shape. 1.x used pre-async/await generator-function middleware (`app.use(function *() { ... })`, `this.body = ...`) -- a REAL, build-verified bucket (not reference-only): confirmed working end-to-end on node:6-slim (generator functions are native from Node 4 onward, no --harmony flag or transpile step needed at that Node floor). Capped at Node 7 since koa 2.x (which needs 8+ regardless, see below) is the better fit for anything newer. Despite the package.json claiming Node >=4, koa 2.x's own dependency tree (`http-errors`, resolved as of the latest 2.x patch) uses object destructuring (`const { HttpError } = require(...)`) that old V8 can't parse -- verified directly: `require("koa")` throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected token {` on node:6-slim, loads fine on node:8-slim. Narrowed to Node 8+.

frameworks.name: Hapi
frameworks.notes: Async server.route()/server.start() API (`Hapi.server({...})`, no request/response params on the handler needed -- `(request, h) => value`). Confirmed working end-to-end on node:22-slim. The package was renamed/rescoped from the unscoped `hapi` to `@hapi/hapi` at this same major (17) -- see `_comment_hapi_legacy` for the pre-rename package's own real 16-major history, which is NOT independently version-bucketed in this pass (a real, disclosed density tradeoff -- see the comment -- not a claim that those versions don't work).

frameworks.name: Restify
frameworks.notes: `restify.createServer()` + `.get(path, handler)`, stable across all 12 tracked majors -- confirmed live only for 11.x (`declared engines: node >=10.0.0`). 11.x's route handlers must be declared `async` (a plain sync handler throws a hard `AssertionError: actual == expected` -- confirmed via a real docker run) -- older majors' exact handler-signature requirement was not independently re-tested per-major in this pass, flagged here rather than silently assumed identical. Compatibility floors for majors 0-8 follow each release's own npm `engines` field where declared; 9+ dropped an explicit engines field and are assumed to track the same Node floor as their contemporaries. THREE real bugs found and fixed 2026-07-13 from a user-reported batch run (node-Restify-20260713-001, 672 failures): (1) `res.json(...)` throws `res.json is not a function` on major "0" (resolves to 0.5.7, restify's actual last-ever 0.x release -- confirmed via the real npm version list -- that ancient release only monkey-patches `http.ServerResponse.prototype.send`, no `res.json()` convenience method exists yet). Fixed by switching every handler to `res.send(200, {...})`, the one call shape confirmed to work across restify's entire tracked history: 0.5.7's `send()` requires a numeric first argument in its legacy positional form (an object with no `.code` property throws `TypeError: options.code must be a number`), while the latest restify (11.x) documents `res.send(201, {hello: 'world'})` as a first-class supported call shape in its own source comments. (2) The unconditional `async (req, res) =>` handler syntax (needed by majors 9-11, which structurally require it -- a plain sync handler throws a hard AssertionError at route-registration time) is a hard `SyntaxError` on Node <7.6, which majors 0-8's own claimed compatibility reaches back into (Node 4-9) -- confirmed via a real docker build+run failure on Node 6. Fixed with a per-major template split (`_RESTIFY_LEGACY_TPL` for majors 0-8, using a plain `(req, res, next) => { ...; return next(); }` handler): there's no Node-version overlap between "major needs async" and "Node can't parse async", so the split is keyed on major, not Node version. (3) Majors 9-11's claimed Node ceiling (26) was wrong, and NOT uniform across the three majors despite their near-identical release dates (Nov 2022-Jan 2023) -- confirmed via a real docker build+run matrix across majors 9/10/11 x Node 16-26: major "9" only runs through Node 16 (Node 18+ crashes with `TypeError: Cannot set property closed of #<Readable> which has only a getter` in restify's own `request.js`, since Node's native `Readable` gained a getter-only `closed` property that restify's older monkey-patch code collides with); majors "10"/"11" fixed that specific collision and run through Node 22, but ALL THREE majors (9/10/11 alike) crash on Node 24/26 with a different, unrelated ceiling: restify's `server.js` unconditionally `require('spdy')` at module-load time (regardless of whether HTTP/2 features are used), which transitively requires `http-deceiver`, which calls the long-removed internal `process.binding('http_parser')` -- `Error: No such module: http_parser`. Narrowed major "9" to `["10"-"16"]`, majors "10"/"11" to `["10"-"22"]` initially -- then a FOURTH bug surfaced from a follow-up real report on Node 10, major "10": `SyntaxError: Unexpected token .` at `sonic-boom/index.js:393` (`cb?.()`, optional-chaining syntax), reached via `pino` (restify's default internal logger, required unconditionally at module-load regardless of whether logging is configured) -- optional chaining needs Node >=14, so majors 10/11's true FLOOR is 14, not 10. Confirmed via a real docker build+run matrix (majors 10/11 x Node 10/12/14/16): both fail identically on 10/12, both work on 14/16. Major "9" does NOT have this problem (confirmed still working on Node 10/12 in the earlier matrix) -- it evidently pins an older pino/sonic-boom (or a different default logger entirely) without optional-chaining syntax, so majors 9 vs 10/11 have DIFFERENT floors despite near-identical release dates, same as their ceilings already weren't uniform either. Final ranges: major "9" `["10"-"16"]`, majors "10"/"11" `["14"-"22"]`. Lesson: don't infer one major's Node floor/ceiling from a sibling major's results just because they share a release-date era and similar dependency-collision failure classes -- majors 9/10/11 needed FOUR independent real-matrix checks (two ceiling failure modes, one floor failure mode, non-uniform across all three) before the picture was actually complete; each apparent "same era, should behave the same" assumption in this investigation turned out wrong at least once. A FIFTH bug, same `spdy` dependency family but a different failure mode: majors "2" (2.8.5) and "3" (3.0.3) depend on ancient `spdy@^1.26.5`, whose `lib/spdy/connection.js` calls `util.inherits(Connection, process.EventEmitter)` -- `process.EventEmitter` (a deprecated global alias for `require('events').EventEmitter`) was removed starting Node 8, confirmed empirically across node:4/6/8/10-slim (`typeof process.EventEmitter`: function on 4/6, undefined on 8/10) -- crashing with `TypeError [ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE]: The "superCtor" argument must be of type Function`. Majors "0" (0.5.7) and "1" (1.4.4) do NOT depend on spdy at all (confirmed via each release's real npm `dependencies` field) and are unaffected -- majors 4-8 already moved to spdy@3.x/4.x (also confirmed via npm) and are a separate, not-yet-independently-verified concern if they turn out to have their own Node-version issues. Narrowed majors "2"/"3" to `["0.10","0.12","4","5","6","7"]` (drop 8-10). Confirmed via real docker build+run: major "3" crashes on Node 8, major "2" works on Node 6.

frameworks.name: NestJS
frameworks.notes: Decorator-based DI/module system (`@Controller`, `@Get`, `@Module`, `NestFactory.create(AppModule)`) -- NOT structurally scaffold-bound despite `nest new`'s prominence in Nest's own docs: `NestFactory.create()` is a documented, first-class standalone bootstrap API. What IS mandatory is a TypeScript compile step: Nest's decorator-based DI reflection depends on TypeScript's own `emitDecoratorMetadata` emission (`design:paramtypes`), which plain V8/Node decorators (the stage-3 proposal, `--experimental-decorators` as a *runtime* flag) do NOT produce -- confirmed the hard way: a hand-written plain-.js version using the runtime flag throws `bad option: --experimental-decorators` on some Node majors and, even where the flag exists, never populates the metadata Nest's DI container needs. Fixed by adding a `tsc` build stage (a new Dockerfile capability for this project's Node generator) -- confirmed working end-to-end on Node 22 (major 11) including real dependency injection and routing. Older majors (1-10) share the same decorator/DI shape and are tracked as real buckets, but only major 11 was independently build-verified this pass. Majors 1-5 marked `available: false` (2026-07-13): fixed several real generator bugs while chasing a user-reported build failure -- `@nestjs/platform-express` doesn't exist before v6 (Nest's 'platform adapter' architecture split Express integration into its own package starting at 6.0.0-alpha.3; before that `@nestjs/core` bundled `express` directly), npm 7+'s strict peer-dependency auto-install needs `--legacy-peer-deps` on both `npm install` AND `npm prune` (two separate command invocations, each re-resolving peers), `typescript` needs a Node-version-tiered pin (`4.9.5` below Node 12, `5.0.4` for Node 12-13, `^5.6.0` for Node >=14 -- newer typescript releases' own bundled JS uses syntax older V8 can't parse), `app.listen(8000, "0.0.0.0")`'s host argument doesn't type-check against pre-v6's `.listen()` overload (fixed by dropping it -- Node's own default already binds all interfaces), and `skipLibCheck` is needed since @nestjs/core's own .d.ts unconditionally references @nestjs/microservices' types. After ALL of those fixes, the app still doesn't BOOT: @nestjs/core@1.0.2 unconditionally `require()`s @nestjs/websockets and @nestjs/microservices at module-load time (confirmed by inspecting the real npm tarball's source, not just its declared peerDependencies) -- these are structurally mandatory in the pre-v6 architecture despite being declared as optional peers, and microservices in turn unconditionally requires `redis`/`json-socket`, websockets requires `socket.io`/`iterare`. Getting all of that installed and mutually version-compatible is possible in principle but was judged not worth pursuing: NestJS 1-5 collectively existed for under 2 years (May 2017 - March 2019) during Nest's earliest, fastest-churning, lowest-adoption period, long before any of its current popularity -- unlike this project's other legacy language/framework versions (old PHP/Python/Java, which have a real decade-plus tail of production usage), there's no realistic population of production systems still running a NestJS major that was itself superseded within months, three major-version bumps before the current architecture even existed. Excluded as a real-world-relevance judgment call, not a technical impossibility (the dependency cascade IS solvable, just not judged worth the ongoing verification burden of also chasing redis/socket.io/json-socket/iterare's OWN Node-version compatibility across this project's full historical Node matrix).

frameworks.name: Sails
frameworks.notes: `sails.lift({ routes: {...} }, callback)` -- a full MVC framework (ORM/blueprints/views/policies/pubsub all present) but genuinely usable standalone: every non-routing hook can be disabled via the `hooks` config option, tested and confirmed working end-to-end on node:22-slim with `hooks: { grunt:false, views:false, session:false, policies:false, orm:false, pubsub:false }`. Only 2 real majors exist total (0.x 2013-2016, 1.x 2018-present, a genuine breaking rewrite) -- both tracked.

frameworks.name: AdonisJS
frameworks.notes: Requires scaffolding, but this project's generator now supports that as a real, build-verified pattern rather than treating 'needs scaffolding' as a reason to exclude it: the Dockerfile runs `npx create-adonisjs@latest . --kit=api` (AdonisJS's own official minimal API starter, non-interactive) and then overwrites `start/routes.ts` with the project's 2 standard routes -- confirmed working end-to-end (both endpoints curled on a live container) on major 6/7's architecture. There is no package named `@adonisjs/core` for majors 3/4 at all (the npm registry jumps 2.x -> 5.x directly, confirmed via the registry API) -- not a gap in this research, AdonisJS itself skipped those numbers. Majors 1/2/5 used an older, different scaffolding CLI (`@adonisjs/cli`'s `adonis new`, not today's `create-adonisjs`) -- tracked as real buckets (a real reason isn't yet established for excluding them) but their own scaffold tool was not independently re-verified this pass, hence no populated compatibility list yet pending that follow-up. Major '6's compatibility was originally (wrongly) extended down to Node 20 -- a real docker build hung INDEFINITELY (confirmed stuck past 10 minutes, never erroring) at the scaffold's own `npm install` step on Node 20, because this project always invokes `create-adonisjs@latest` (currently 3.4.0), and that latest release's own package.json declares `engines: {"node": ">=24.0.0"}` (surfaced directly in the build log's `npm warn EBADENGINE` before the hang). Confirmed the fix by rerunning the identical Dockerfile on `node:24-slim`: completes cleanly in ~60s. Narrowed to `["24","25","26"]`, matching major 7. Since this registry always scaffolds via `@latest` rather than a pinned create-adonisjs version, major 6's Node floor is really THIS project's floor for invoking today's create-adonisjs, not major 6 (`@adonisjs/core`) itself's historical floor -- re-check this note if create-adonisjs's own engines requirement ever drops back down.

cryptography_libs.name: crypto
cryptography_libs.notes: Node.js standard library crypto module (OpenSSL-backed) — always available.

cryptography_libs.name: node-forge
cryptography_libs.notes: Pure-JS TLS/crypto implementation (RSA, AES, legacy hashes). Popular pre-WebCrypto compatibility shim. Package.json claims 1.x needs only Node >=6.13.0, but that's wrong: 1.0.0's lib/log.js unconditionally calls the global `URLSearchParams` (added in Node 10.0.0) at require-time whenever `console` exists -- guaranteed crash on Node <10 (verified against the actual npm tarball). Fixed upstream by 1.4.0 (query-var block now gated behind `typeof window !== 'undefined'`, so it's a no-op under Node). 1.0's compat therefore starts at 10, not 6.

cryptography_libs.name: tweetnacl
cryptography_libs.notes: Pure-JS port of the NaCl/libsodium cryptography (box/secretbox/sign, no KEM/PQC). Zero dependencies, plain ES5 -- confirmed working via `require('tweetnacl')` + `nacl.box.keyPair()` on node:22-slim; nothing in the source uses any post-ES5 syntax, so no Node-version floor beyond what npm itself needs. Unmaintained since 1.0.3 (2020) but still a common legacy dependency, tracked as an SCA reference point the same way crypto-js is.

cryptography_libs.name: node-jose
cryptography_libs.notes: Cisco's JOSE/JWK implementation -- a genuinely DIFFERENT package from 'jose' (panva) already tracked above, not a duplicate/rename (confirmed: distinct npm packages, distinct authors, distinct APIs -- node-jose exposes a `JWK.asKey()`/keystore-object model backed by Node's own WebCrypto/crypto module, 'jose' is a newer zero-dependency functional API). Confirmed working via `require('node-jose').JWK` on node:22-slim.

cryptography_libs.name: jose
cryptography_libs.notes: JWT/JWS/JWE/JWK implementation. v3+ stopped declaring 'engines' in package.json; minimums here follow the project's documented Node support policy per major.

cryptography_libs.name: crypto-js
cryptography_libs.notes: Dependency-free pure-JS classic crypto (MD5, SHA, AES, DES, RC4). No declared Node engine requirement in either major — included as an SCA weak/legacy-algorithm reference point. 4.x's blowfish.js (and likely other cipher files) use bare `let`/`const` with no "use strict" pragma -- verified by actually building/running crypto-js@4.2.0 against real node:4/6/8-slim containers: throws `SyntaxError: Block-scoped declarations ... not yet supported outside strict mode` on Node 4 (old V8 required strict mode for block-scoped declarations), works from Node 6 onward. 3.x (verified same way with 3.3.0) is plain ES5 and works fine down to Node 4.

cryptography_libs.name: sodium-native
cryptography_libs.notes: Native libsodium bindings (N-API + prebuildify) — the only planned library needing a C toolchain (node-gyp: python3/make/g++, plus autoconf/automake/libtool for the from-source libsodium build used when no prebuilt binary exists for the exact Node ABI, e.g. old sodium-native major x very new Node major) in the image. v5 primarily targets the Bare runtime but still supports Node 18+. v3's node-gyp fallback build script requires the 'fs/promises' subpath (only importable as its own module since Node 14, though the fs.promises property existed since Node 10) — bumped to 14+ after a real build failure on Node 10.

cryptography_libs.name: bcrypt
cryptography_libs.notes: Native node-gyp binding around OpenBSD's bcrypt (password hashing, not general-purpose encryption). Confirmed working via `bcrypt.hashSync()` on node:22-slim with python3/make/g++ installed for node-gyp's prebuild fallback. Prebuilt binaries exist for most current-Node/major combos, falling back to source compile otherwise -- same sys_deps class as sodium-native. The `0` bucket's compatibility was originally (wrongly) claimed down to Node 0.10/4 -- a real docker build failure on Node 10 surfaced TWO distinct genuine ceilings once node-gyp's Python-lookup was fixed (via `ENV PYTHON=python3`): Node 4/6 hit an unfixable ceiling in the bundled node-gyp itself (v3.4.0, structurally Python-2-only -- 'Python executable ... is v3.5.3, which is not supported by gyp ... pass --python switch to point to Python >= v2.5.0 & < 3.0.0'), while Node 9/10 hit a *different* genuine ceiling in bcrypt 0.8.7's own vendored `nan.h`: V8's `Object::ForceSet` was removed by that point ('class v8::Object has no member named ForceSet'). Node 8 confirmed fully working end-to-end (build + live `bcrypt.hashSync()` via /version, bcrypt@0.8.7 on Node v8.17.0) -- narrowed to just `["8"]` since it's the only currently-tracked major that survives both ceilings. Buckets `1` (1.0.3) and `2` (2.0.1) needed the same real-floor narrowing, each for its own reason, only found by testing every included (even/LTS) Node major in its claimed range: `1` only has prebuilt binaries for Node 6/8's ABIs -- Node 4 falls back to source and hits the same Python-2-only node-gyp ceiling as bucket `0`, and Node 10/12 fall back to source and hit a genuine bug in the old bundled `node-pre-gyp@0.6.36` itself (confirmed via a real docker build: its 'Pre-built binaries not found... falling back to source compile' fallback path fires *twice* concurrently for the same target, both `make` invocations racing on the same intermediate `.d.raw` dependency file -- 'sed: can't read .../blowfish.o.d.raw', 'fatal error: opening dependency file .../bcrypt.o.d.raw' -- confirmed this is NOT a make -j parallelism issue by setting `ENV JOBS=1`, which made no difference; the double invocation comes from node-pre-gyp's own install-script logic, not from make's job scheduler). Narrowed bucket `1` to `["6","8"]`. Bucket `2` has prebuilt binaries through Node 10, but Node 12/14 fall back to source compile and hit a *third*, different genuine ceiling: 2.0.1's vendored `nan` headers use V8 APIs removed by that point (`v8::Function::New` old signature, `v8::Value::ToString()` no-arg overload, `Nan::Persistent::IsNearDeath`) -- same failure class as bucket `0`'s ForceSet ceiling, different specific APIs. Narrowed bucket `2` to `["6","8","10"]`. Bucket `3` (3.0.x) was re-verified on Node 6/12/14 and needs no changes -- its vendored `nan` is new enough to already handle the V8 APIs that break bucket `2`.

cryptography_libs.name: bcryptjs
cryptography_libs.notes: Pure-JS bcrypt implementation, zero native dependencies -- the deliberate pure-JS/native pairing with `bcrypt` above (same pattern as sodium-native vs crypto-js). Confirmed working via `bcryptjs.hashSync()` on node:22-slim. A `0.x` line technically exists in npm's own metadata but its registry timestamp (2025) postdates 1.x/2.x/3.x, an npm publish-history anomaly (likely a deprecated-and-relisted old tag) rather than real chronological history -- not tracked as its own bucket pending clarification.

cryptography_libs.name: argon2
cryptography_libs.notes: Native node-gyp binding around the Argon2 password-hashing reference implementation -- has never left the 0.x line (0.1.1 through 0.44.0, all still major '0'), so only one real bucket exists; this registry always resolves to the latest 0.x patch. Confirmed working via `await argon2.hash('x')` on node:22-slim with the same node-gyp toolchain as bcrypt/sodium-native. Compatibility was originally (wrongly) claimed all the way down to Node 4 -- a real docker build failure on Node 10 surfaced two of argon2 0.44.0's own UNPINNED transitive dependencies each requiring modern Node: `cross-env@10.1.0` needs Node >=20 (its own bin script uses ESM `import` syntax, a hard `SyntaxError` on anything older) and `node-addon-api@8.9.0` needs Node >=18 (its headers use `std::string_view`, C++17, which node-gyp only compiles with on Node 18+'s default toolchain -- confirmed via a real docker build: Node 16 fails with `'std::string_view' has not been declared`, Node 14 fails earlier still). Bisected directly with this project's own real generated Dockerfiles (not a bare npm install): 14/16 fail, 18/20 confirmed working end-to-end (build + live /version). Corrected to `["18","20","22","24","26"]` -- the same unpinned-transitive-dependency class already hit for aiohttp/async-timeout (Python) and Iris/Echo's fasthttp/gommon (Go), just resolved by narrowing the registry instead of pinning, since (unlike aiohttp) there's only one argon2 bucket to begin with -- no older 'era' combo worth preserving by force-pinning a moving-target build tool.

cryptography_libs.name: liboqs-node
cryptography_libs.notes: The Node.js equivalent of Go's liboqs-go / PHP's php-liboqs / .NET's LibOQS.NET -- a native N-API binding wrapping liboqs via liboqs-cpp, community-maintained (TapuCosmo), explicitly marked EXPERIMENTAL by its own README ('do not use in production'). Only 4 patch releases ever published (0.0.1-0.1.0), all under a single major -- one real bucket. FIVE real build issues found and fixed/diagnosed, none anticipated from the README: (1) the npm-published tarball does NOT include its `deps/liboqs`/`deps/liboqs-cpp` git submodules (npm doesn't capture submodule content), and no prebuilt binary exists for modern Node ABIs either -- a plain `npm install liboqs-node` fails outright; fixed by git-cloning the repo directly with `--recurse-submodules` in the Dockerfile (the same 'clone from source, don't rely on the package manager' pattern as php-liboqs) rather than depending on the npm registry tarball at all. (2) the vendored liboqs commit (pinned by this package, from ~2021) fails to compile under a modern GCC (12+): its old SIKE implementation trips `-Werror=array-parameter`/`-Werror=stringop-overflow`, warning classes added to GCC after this liboqs commit was written -- fixed by stripping `-Werror` from every CMakeLists.txt/*.cmake file under the vendored `deps/liboqs` tree before building (SIKE itself was cryptographically broken and removed from liboqs entirely in 2022, well after this pinned commit -- not a correctness concern here, purely a compiler-strictness mismatch). (3) because of that same old pinned liboqs commit, only DRAFT algorithm names are exposed (`Kyber768`, not `ML-KEM-768`) -- confirmed live via `oqs.KEMs.getEnabledAlgorithms()`; the touch code must use the draft name, unlike every other language's liboqs binding in this project which already exposes final NIST names. (4) this package's OWN npm install script is silently skipped by npm's root-safety guard (a package refusing to run its own lifecycle install script as root without explicit opt-in) -- `npm install` printed a WARN and exited 0 without ever compiling the native binding, only surfacing as a runtime 'Could not locate the bindings file' crash; fixed with `--unsafe-perm` on that specific install. (5) compatibility was originally (wrongly) claimed down to Node 12 -- bisected with this project's own real generated Dockerfiles across every tracked major from 12 to 22: Node 12/13 hit a genuine ceiling (liboqs's CMake requires OpenSSL >=1.1.1, but Node 12's Debian base only ships 1.1.0l), Node 14-17 hit a *different* genuine ceiling (a C++ compiler-strictness/version mismatch in liboqs-cpp's own vendored `KEMs.cpp`: 'qualified-id in declaration before ')' token', a GCC-version-specific parse error, not present on the newer GCC bundled with Node 18+'s Debian base). Node 18/20/22 all confirmed fully working end-to-end (build + live /version, real Kyber768 keypair generated) -- narrowed to `["18"-"26"]`.

cryptography_libs.name: @noble/curves
cryptography_libs.notes: Auditable pure-JS elliptic curves (secp256k1, ed25519, NIST curves). Sibling project to @noble/post-quantum, same author (paulmillr). 2.x requires Node >=20.19.0 (reduces to major 20 at our granularity).

cryptography_libs.name: @noble/post-quantum
cryptography_libs.notes: Auditable pure-JS ML-KEM/ML-DSA/SLH-DSA/FN-DSA (FIPS 203/204/205 + Falcon). The Node.js equivalent of Go's circl/liboqs-go — primary PQC library for this language.

languages._comment_http_clients: New axis for the client-fingerprinting experiment (see registry python.json's own _comment_http_clients for the full design rationale) -- outbound HTTP(S) call generators, not servers. Mirrors each cryptography_libs entry's version history into one or more client counterparts. Only node-forge gets a -raw variant (the only Node crypto library with its own TLS implementation distinct from the built-in OpenSSL-backed tls module) -- every other entry is sign-only, attaching an X-Signature header built with that library's own natural primitive.

http_clients.name: crypto-hmac
http_clients.notes: HMAC-SHA256 over the probe message via Node's built-in crypto module, shared secret. Mirrors Python's hashlib-hmac -- the lowest-bar reference point.

http_clients.name: node-forge-raw
http_clients.notes: Drives node-forge's own pure-JS TLS implementation (forge.tls) directly over a raw net.Socket against the target's :9443 -- the only Node crypto library with its own TLS stack distinct from Node's OpenSSL-backed built-in tls module, mirroring pyOpenSSL-raw/M2Crypto-raw. ALL VERSIONS marked `available: false` and no longer get client images (2026-07-18): a real fingerprint capture against fingerprint_target fails with a `protocol_version` TLS alert on every version -- confirmed via node-forge's own source (`lib/tls.js`) that `tls.SupportedVersions` only ever contains TLS_1_0/TLS_1_1; `tls.Versions.TLS_1_2` is defined but referenced nowhere else in the file, meaning TLS 1.2 protocol support was simply never implemented. fingerprint_target's default Python `ssl.SSLContext(PROTOCOL_TLS_SERVER)` requires TLS 1.2+, so the two can never negotiate a shared version, on any node-forge release. Same permanent-impossibility category as pyOpenSSL 0.15.1 and jose-sign majors 1-3 above.

http_clients.name: node-forge-sign
http_clients.notes: RSA-2048 PSS/SHA-256 via forge.pki/forge.pss over plain HTTP(S) transport -- same algorithm choice as cryptography-sign (Python) on purpose, for cross-language negative-control comparison.

http_clients.name: node-jose-sign
http_clients.notes: Compact JWS via node-jose's JWK/JWS API -- a genuinely different package from 'jose' below (Cisco's keystore-object model vs panva's functional API), tracked separately.

http_clients.name: jose-sign
http_clients.notes: Compact JWS via jose's functional CompactSign API -- mirrors authlib-jwt's cleartext-alg-announcing shape. ONLY MAJORS 4, 5 AND 6 WORK. Majors 1, 2 and 3 are marked `available: false` and no longer get client images -- they will always fail with "CompactSign is not a constructor", this is expected, not a bug. Why: the 'jose' library completely replaced its own signing API partway through its history. This script uses jose's current/modern way of signing (a class called CompactSign). Majors 1, 2 and 3 don't have that class at all -- they used a different, older signing method that this script doesn't support. To make 1-3 work too, someone would need to write a second version of this script specifically for that old method -- not done yet, kept here as a known, deliberate gap.

http_clients.name: crypto-js-sign
http_clients.notes: HMAC-SHA256 via CryptoJS -- same algorithm as crypto-hmac, a JS-library-vs-built-in comparison point.

http_clients.name: sodium-native-sign
http_clients.notes: Ed25519 via sodium-native's crypto_sign primitives -- mirrors pynacl-sign (both libsodium-backed).

http_clients.name: bcrypt-sign
http_clients.notes: bcrypt hash of the probe message + shared secret (distinctive $2b$ header shape) -- native node-gyp binding.

http_clients.name: bcryptjs-sign
http_clients.notes: Same construction as bcrypt-sign, pure-JS implementation -- no native toolchain needed.

http_clients.name: argon2-sign
http_clients.notes: argon2id hash of the probe message + shared secret (distinctive $argon2id$ header shape).

http_clients.name: liboqs-node-sign
http_clients.notes: ML-DSA/Dilithium3 signature (draft algorithm name -- this project's liboqs-node binding only exposes pre-standardization names, see the server-side cryptography_libs entry's own notes) -- mirrors liboqs-sign.

http_clients.name: noble-curves-sign
http_clients.notes: ed25519/secp256k1 signature via @noble/curves -- mirrors ecdsa-sign. Major 0.1's own script genuinely fails (`ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED`) -- confirmed live via the real published 0.1.0 tarball's package.json: its exports map only has './modular', './shortw', './utils', no ed25519 submodule at all yet (same pattern as this file's own lang_node.py already documents for secp256k1's later addition). Left as a genuine, accepted historical-reference row rather than building a bespoke Weierstrass-based signature just for this one early bucket.

http_clients.name: noble-pq-sign
http_clients.notes: ML-DSA signature via @noble/post-quantum -- a second, independent PQC-native library alongside liboqs-node-sign.
